responseSet.title: The Mystery of Things: Christopher Bollas, New York: Routledge, 1999, 203 pp., $29.99.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Mystery of Things

Christopher Bollas, New York: Routledge, 1999, 203 pp., $29.99.

Eileen Kohutis PHD

Eileen Kohutis

Eileen Kohutis  

In his latest book, The Mystery of Things, Christopher Bollas argues that the importance of free association in psychoanalytic technique and its application to Western thought has never been fully explicated. To remedy this oversight, he presents his theory of free association in three sections in this book. The first section is a theoretical critique of psychoanalytic theory; the second section contains clinical case material illustrating how free association and interpretation change psychic structure; the last section is a compendium of various essays dealing with creativity. This is a heroic task given the small size of this book (only 195 pages of text). Almost half of the chapters have already been published as articles or book chapters in various psychoanalytic publications and cover a variety of topics, such as language, sexuality and the body, and the arts. Although this masterful clinician discusses these compelling topics, he does not present the unifying thread showing how these subjects relate to free association until the final three pages of the text. Unfortunately, this hinders its relevance because the reader is uncertain of the relationship of chapters.

With his inimitable writing style, Bollas is at times turgid and abstruse, eloquent and seductive.

Eileen A. Kohutis, PhD, independent practice, Livingston, New Jersey; Institute for Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, South Orange, New Jersey; and St. Barnabas Medical Center, Livingston, New Jersey.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blaming the Brain: The Truth About Drugs and Mental Health

Elliot S. Valenstein, New York: Free Press, 1998, 292 pp., $25.00.

Louis Berger PHD

Louis Berger

Louis Berger  

Valenstein is Professor Emeritus of Psychology and Neuroscience at the University of Michigan, a biopsychologist who spent over 40 years studying “how the brain and other biological factors such as hormones and drugs influence behavior” (p. 6). He is not a psychotherapist, and in principle he is not against the idea of viewing and treating mental disorders from within a biological framework. (He does believe, though, that there is a need for a psychosocial component also—more of that later.)

Originally he had set out to write a history of the interplay between the accidental discoveries of psychotherapeutic drugs and the set of scientific, psychosocial, and economic factors that within only a few decades had led to the drastic, wide-spread changes from practices that reflected a more or less psychodynamic orientation to those arising from the conviction that chemical events in the brain are “not only the cause of mental disorders, but also the explanation of the normal variations in personality and behavior” (p. 1).

As he gathered material for his project, however, Valenstein noticed a recurring general pattern of scientifically and medically unacceptable work apparently abetted by a constellation of shoddy motives. Consequently, he expanded his plan to include questions concerning evaluation and motivation, such as: Does the evidence support the received views concerning the biological causes of disorders? Does it support the new biochemical theories of mental illnesses and the drug treatment approaches that evolved in the context of these theories?

Louis S. Berger, PhD, independent practice, San Antonio, Texas.

Sorting out the distinctions between reality and fantasy in terms of apparently recovered memories and reconstructions is at least as puzzling as sorting out current realities. In responding to C. B. Brenneis's (1997, 2000) challenge to the existence of the phenomena of repression and of recovered or reconstructed memories, the authors point out the data from the Recovered Memory Archive web site, the data from World War I and World War II battlefield neuroses, as well as the clinical observations of psychoanalysts.

responseSet.title: What I Do With What You Give Me: Therapeutic Action as the Creation of Meaning
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What I Do With What You Give Me

Therapeutic Action as the Creation of Meaning

Frank Summers PHD

Frank Summers

Frank Summers  

The thesis of this article is that the essence of analytic change lies not in what the analyst offers, but in what the patient creates from the analyst's provisions, whether these offerings are interpretations, functions, or an emotional impact. This concept of analytic change implies a clinical strategy in which interpretations are viewed as necessary preparations. The analyst's role is not only to understand, but also to facilitate the patient's creative use of interpretation by opening a therapeutic space. The inclusion of the patient's creation of new meaning in the theory of therapeutic action implies a clinical strategy in which the analyst facilitates the patient's creation. In such an analytic stance, the analyst opens therapeutic space for the patient to create new possiblities. A clinical illustration demonstrates a strategy that combines discovery and the promotion of the patient's creation of new meaning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Empathy's Romantic Dialectic

Self Psychology, Intersubjectivity, and Imagination

David Klugman CSW

David Klugman

David Klugman  

The author views Kohut's conceptualization of psychoanalytic empathy and its subsequent development by intersubjectivity theorists as an extension of a larger Romantic epistemological tradition in which the role of imagination in mental life is both central and precise. To illuminate this argument, the author reconsiders Kohut's distinction between the “presence of empathy” and “empathy as a mode of observation.” Next is described the way in which the ambivalence represented by this distinction is resolved through intersubjectivity theory. Finally, the author explores several key aspects of the Romantic imagination as a response to Cartesianism in order to evolve an understanding of empathy as a bilateral procedure mediating self-experience and experience of the other.

responseSet.title: Revolutions in Psychoanalytic Theory of Lesbian Development: Dora to Dykes and Back Again
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revolutions in Psychoanalytic Theory of Lesbian Development

Dora to Dykes and Back Again

Michelle Jacobo PHD

Michelle Jacobo

Michelle Jacobo  

Nowhere is the therapeutic action in psychoanalytic theory, away from Freud and back again, more striking than in the realm of homosexuality. Theories of lesbian development have evolved to appreciate the complexity of sexuality and gender as expressed in sexual object choice, foregrounded within psychoanalysis by proponents of relational and intersubjectivity theories. In this article, I argue that it was not until psychoanalysis came to embrace this complex and contextualized understanding of homosexuality that therapists could become curious with patients about conflict-based aspects of their homosexual selves without risk of pathologizing. I elaborate the ways in which contemporary psychoanalytic theories of lesbian development have provided a theoretical context from which therapists may allow ideas of drive, conflict, and object relations to inform and to advance work with their lesbian patients.

responseSet.title: The Talking Cure: About Language
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Talking Cure

About Language

William Glover PHD

William Glover

William Glover  

This article reviews the panel held at the 20th Annual Spring Meeting, Division of Psychoanalysis (39), American Psychological Association, April 6, 2000, San Francisco. The panelists were Judith butler and Adrienne Harris, and the moderator was Jeanne Wolf Bernstein.

Adrienne Harris, in her paper “Trance and Transference: Linguistic and Identificatory Aspects of transference Phenomena,” criticized the distinction often made by psychoanalysts between words and acts and the subsequent valuing of words over acts. In dividing speech from action, a mind-body split is perpetuated. Harris argued that language, or language that communicates effectively, is very much a bodily experience. Talk that cures is talk that closes the gap between speech and act. Citing the work of vygotsky, the speech act theorist Bahktin, and the contemporary linguists Lakoff and Johnson, Harris asserted that it is because of its physicality that speech cures.

This bodily materiality of language is expressed in what Harris termed the trance aspect of transference.

William C. Glover, PhD, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute, San Francisco, California.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Musical Edge of Therapeutic Dialogue

Steven H. Knoblauch, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 2000, 175 pp., $33.50.

Alexander Stein MSSc

Alexander Stein

New York

Alexander Stein  

The central thesis of Steven H. Knoblauch's slender new book The Musical Edge of Therapeutic Dialogue is that “attention to the dimensions of the musical edge gives the analyst a broadened scope for perceiving meaning: the analyst's responsiveness … may be critically shaped by the nonverbal dimensions of his actions” (p. 80).

knoblauch elaborates this nonverbal dimension primarily in intersubjectivist terms against a metaphoric backdrop of music, most particularly jazz improvisation. To Knoblauch, the jazz improviser,

using only the nonverbal contours of sound, is moved by and moves those with whom she interacts, other musicians, and listeners through attention to, and participation with, the tones and rhythms on this edge of experience. Her solo represents the shaping and reshaping of sound contours as call and response to the matching and mismatching, tension-building and resolving, space-opening and closing signals being exchanged with those around her. (p. 160)

Continuing, he writes that “similarly, therapist and patient, often unconsciously but sometimes consciously, are influencing and intersecting their subjective experience of each other with the music of their utterances” (p. 160).

Alexander Stein, MSSc, New School University and independent practice. New York. New York.

Who's that Girl? Who's that Boy? Clinical Practice Meets Postmodern Gender Theory, by Lynne Layton, Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1999, 267 pp., $30.00.

Richard Reichbart PHD

Richard Reichbart

Richard Reichbart  

Arising from the area of gender and sexuality, a revolution is taking place within psychoanalysis, one that has been developing for years and has recently burst forth more fully. It has spawned a landmark 1996 supplementary volume of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association; two new journals on gender and sexuality; and innumerable books, presentations, and articles. Generally, established institutes seem unaware of this revolution in a formal sense and have yet to change their required curriculums to accommodate it. Many male psychoanalysis, to judge by their lack of attendance at psychology-of-women or gender-related presentations or study groups, appear resistant, unaware that these changes will ultimately affect not only the way in which psychoanalysts think about women but about men.

Richard Reichbart, PhD, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, New York, New York.

responseSet.title: Disembodied Intimacies: Identity and relationship on the Internet
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disembodied Intimacies

Identity and relationship on the Internet

Judith Hanlon PHD

Judith Hanlon

Judith Hanlon  

This panel explored the psychological impact of today's expanding computer technology. Speakers addressed such phenomena as computer-mediated relationships, experimentation with different aspects of self via the Internet, and changes in children's play due to the new technology. The impact of the digital revolution on psychoanalytic treatment itself was also explored, including the prospect of computer-mediated therapy.

Sections I and V joined forces this April at the Division 39 Meetings to sponsor a panel titled “Disembodied Intimacies: Identity and Relationship on the Internet.” The panel was a beginning attempt to understand the psychological impact of the meteoric expansion of computer technology in our age. It addressed such questions as How does the new technology change the nature of human relationships? How does it shape the experience of self? What signs of these new developments are therapists seeing in their practices? In the future, how is the digital revolution likely to affect psychoanalytic practice itself?

After an introduction to the panel by Elliot Adler, Helen silverman gave the first presentation.

Judith Hanlon, PhD, Columbia University Health Service.

responseSet.title: Signal Anxiety From the Adaptive Point of View
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Signal Anxiety From the Adaptive Point of View

Eric Gillett MD

Eric Gillett

Kentfield, California

Eric Gillett  

The adaptive importance of anxiety is obvious from its role in the avoidance of danger. Attenuation of the anxiety response is adaptive because extreme anxiety may impede effective coping behavior and because extreme anxiety has harmful physiological effects. This attenuation has traditionally been explained in terms of “taming,” “structuralization,” and “turning passive into active.” The present article argued for the explanation provided by the double-prediction theory over traditional explanations because of its logical connection to the concept of the impulse-defense equilibrium. An innate mechanism is postulated that communicates information on the status of the impulse-defense balance to the censor.

Prejudice as a Function of Self-Organization

Martha Ryan PsyD and Peter Buirski PHD

Peter Buirski

Peter Buirski  

This article reviewed the traditional psychoanalytic theories of the development and maintenance of prejudiced attitudes and affects. To this body of understanding, the authors offered a self psychological perspective. They described the treatment of Sandy, a woman who presented with extreme expressions of prejudice, to illustrate how self psychology provides a framework for understanding the narcissistic roots of her prejudice and a therapeutic stance for promoting the transformation of her prejudiced attitudes and affects. From this perspective, prejudice is understood not as the displacement or projection of aggression, but as an expression of a vulnerable, fragmentation-prone self-organization struggling to overcome a traumatic developmental history.

The Jungians: A Comparative and Historical Perspective

Thomas B. Kirsch, London: Routledge, 2000, 276 pp., $49.95

Nathan Hale PHD

Nathan Hale

Nathan Hale  

This is a surprising, well written, and even-handed account of an important but relatively little known historical movement, the dissemination of Carl Jung's ideas around the world from Switzerland to New Zealand. Did anyone who was not an insider know, for instance, that one third of all Jungian analysts were and are Jews? None of those Jewish Jungians who knew Jung or worked with him during the period of his peculiar, brief flirtation with Hitler's Germany in the early 1930s experienced the slightest hint of anti-Semitism from him. Or was it widely known that Jung's ideas flourished in Italy and particularly impressed several important scholars of the Orient there. Even more surprising, Freud's first Italian follower, Edouardo Weiss, a Jew, became a Jungian and then migrated to Freud's Chicago Psychoanalytic Institute. Finally, there are remarkable similarities to the history of Freud's psychoanalytic movement as well as some unique characteristics of Jung and his followers.

Typical of Jung's movement were Jews and gentiles alike who shared a passionate interest in religion and mysticism, not as pathology or wish fulfillment, but as enduring and valid aspects of the human psyche.

Nathan G. Hale Jr., PhD, Emeritus, Department of History, University of California.

Nancy J. Chodorow, New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 1999, 320 pp., $27.50

Lucy Zabarenko PHD

Lucy Zabarenko

Lucy Zabarenko  

From Nancy Chodorow, feminist, sociologist, analyst and pungent commentator on psychoanalytic conceptualization comes her latest salvo: The Power of Feelings: Personal Meaning in Psychoanalysis, Gender and Culture. Analyst–readers unacquainted with her work are in for treats like a benevolent humbling, inviting bafflements, and extirpation of any tendency to smugness or hidebound thought. Those familiar with Chodorow will appreciate the update since Femininities, Masculinities, Sexualities: Freud and Beyond (Chodorow, 1994) and “Theoretical Gender and Clinical Gender” (Chodorow, 1996), her contribution to the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association Supplement, “The Psychology of Women.” This latest collection of essays is in four sections: Psychoanalysis, Gender, Culture, and Conclusions, with the heaviest emphasis, almost 40% of the text, on culture.

Trying to keep track of her unfolding views is a little like Alice chasing the rabbit: Chodorow's reasoning is fraught with swift, unexpected turnings, and just when you might think you have locked onto a direction, she will turn a corner and you need to scurry to catch up. It makes for fascinating reading. Furthermore, this is a voice that does not spring from the usual disciplines that inform analytic scholarship: medicine, psychology, or the style of nineteenth century natural science.

Lucy M. Zabarenko, PhD, Faculty, Pittsburgh Psychoanalytic Institute, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

Psychoanalytic Therapy and the Gay Man

Jack Drescher, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 1998, 373 pp., $55.00

Kenneth Lewes PHD

Kenneth Lewes

New York, New York

Kenneth Lewes  

For the first 80 years of its history, psychoanalysis was engaged in the self-imposed project of converting homosexuality. The past 20 years, however, has witnessed a startling turnabout: the transformation of psychoanalysis itself by homosexuality along with feminism and the literary ideas associated with modern hermeneutics and deconstruction. Gay people and their supporters have argued successfully to have this sexual orientation recognized as the full equivalent of the more common hetero-sexuality, and, in doing so, they have forced a reassessment and reformulation of basic psychoanalytic principles and procedures. This represents an extraordinary moment in intellectual and social history, and Jack Drescher's Psychoanalytic Therapy and the Gay Man may be considered its monument. It is the objective marker, the celebration and the concrete embodiment of the shift that has unsettled and reestablished psychoanalysis. Because it is the monument of this moment, it offers us the opportunity to take the measure of how far we have come, what still needs to be done, what we have gained, and what we have lost.

The book itself is a pleasure to read. It is gracefully written and blessedly free of jargon, obfuscation, grammatical errors, and pomposity. Drescher's tone is remarkably humane, and although he can be quite stern when he deals with what seem to him to be moral issues, he projects a winning sense of humor and a sober respect for his patients and his readers. Similarly, he disagrees with other clinicians (including the present reviewer) in a respectful though sometimes exasperated and sardonic tone, and only rarely does he descend to bad manners (e.g., p. 159).

Kenneth Lewes, PhD, independent practice, New York, New York.

How Will Psychoanalysis Integrate New Scientific Developments?

Adam Kremen PHD

Adam Kremen

Oakland, California

Adam Kremen  

This report summarizes a panel (M. Murphy, 2000) that addressed the implications of new directions in science for psychoanalysis and for therapeutic technique. Elizabeth Mayer discussed empirical and theoretical developments in the sciences that point to a connected quality of physical reality. The notion of a connected or participatory universe has far-reaching implications for understanding what takes place in the clinical encounter. David Brin discussed the need for theories of human nature to consider the concept of emergent properties, the tendency of complex systems to reorganize themselves in ways that cannot be predicted. He discussed a recent emergent property of the personality system, the defiance of authority, which he described as a societal immune system against error. Both panelists stressed the need for open-mindedness in psychoanalytic conceptions of reality and of human nature.

Maureen Murphy, the moderator, opened the panel by noting that new and forthcoming developments in the biological sciences promise to tell us much about our origins, our history, and our future, as well as to influence who we are and who we become.

Adam M. Kremen, PhD. independent practice, Oakland, California.

Lucy Zabarenko

Lucy Zabarenko  

At a time when more and more is being written on the topic of learning disabilities, the scope of interdisciplinary interest is widening, and diagnostic refinements for the disorder are proliferating, it's a pleasure to find a chronicle of psychoanalytic contributions to the field. Learning Disabilities and Psychic Conflict: A Psychoanalytic Casebook is a fine, stout book, buttressed with excellent scholarship and thoughtful clinical data and so skillfully organized that readers can easily sample the material in several dimensions.

Delivering exactly what the title promises, the authors have assembled 9 extensive case histories of patients with learning disabilities from 8 psychoanalysts; 1 anonymous contributor has written about 2 patients. The ages of the patients range from five children aged 5 to 8 at the beginning of treatment to one adolescent and three adults. Each account is followed by the authors' thorough critiques, and the volume is bracketed with 2 introductory chapters, a discussion of “Clinical, Theoretical, and Technical Issues in the Analysis of Patients with Learning Disabilities” (p. 9) and a review of the literature (pp. 37) and closes with a tightly reasoned conclusion (p. 461). Six of the contributors are physicians, one a psychologist, and one a social worker, and of the seven identified contributors, six are listed as active members of the American Psychoanalytic Association, and three are certified in child analysis.

Lucy M. Zabarenko, PhD, Pittsburgh Psychoanalytic Institute, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, and School of Medicine, University of Pittsburgh.

Although this is the third and final volume of the Freud-Ferenczi letters, researchers are still at an early stage of understanding Freud as a correspondent. Even though so far there are over a dozen books of Freud's letters in English, there is still much more to come in the future: For example, the long-awaited correspondence with Freud's future wife Martha has still not been published. And other fresh books of Freud letters can be expected to be steadily appearing over the next couple of decades. Further, new editions of Freud's already published letters to Karl Abraham, Lou Andreas-Salomé, Oscar Pfister, and Arnold Zweig are required because unfortunately the original editions of these four correspondences were expurgated on no consistent principles. Although a future generation will be able to be more secure in its assessment of Freud's letter-writing, I think that the Freud–Ferenczi correspondence, and in particular this most recent volume, will continue to be of outstanding interest. Drive drives the subject beyond his own boundaries. As long as it is merely a matter of desire, life is a bed of roses, there is laughter and tears and, above all, talk.

—Verhaeghe (1999, pp. 168-169)

The poster ad for the controversial 1999 film American Beauty, by director Sam Mendes, shows a partial view of the naked torso of a young woman holding a red rose against her belly. Her nails are perfectly manicured. The soft, pale color of her skin is in striking contrast to her red lipstick and red rose. The beautifully designed poster is composed of part-objects, and lacks social features or any reference to subjectivity. The bystander may mistake the poster for a Madison Avenue cosmetic ad. This is American Beauty, skin-deep part-object for sale in the supermarket of desire.

The film displays a cynical and hypercritical view of a culture fascinated with images and their correlates—materialism and consumerism. Staged in the late 20th century, when shopping in the temple-mall has achieved the status of a quasireligious experience, the narrative mirrors a fable for mature audiences. Regret and ultimately redemption follow sin, and transgressors of established boundaries do not go unpunished.

American Beauty belongs to a breed of contemporary films that challenge traditional images of masculinity. Critics and movie analysts have referred to this challenge as a “recreation of masculinity” (Pollack, 1993). Male heroes in films have become softer if not weak, unable or unwilling to make important decisions in their lives, at times trapped in an objectification once applied to women. [[RunningHead]]

Freud, Psychoanalysis and Symbolism

Agnes Petocz, Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press, 1999, 284 pp., $59.95.

Nigel Mackay DPhil

Nigel Mackay

Nigel Mackay  

Bertalanffy (1981) wrote that although “symbolic activity is one of the most fundamental manifestations of the human mind, scientific psychology has in no way given the problem the attention it deserves” (p. 42). Petocz points out that disciplines—from social science, philosophy, semiotics, aesthetics, and elsewhere—that do claim symbolism as their subject matter offer neither a unified nor a scientific theory of the symbol. They commonly view the symbol as in its nature intractable and not amenable to investigation by science. (Ironically, a similar view has kept mainstream empirical psychology away from symbolism.) The concept of the symbol, like that of meaning to which it is intimately related, is set about with confusion, even mystique, that interferes with its clear analysis and systematic explanation.

This sets two tasks for Petocz: first, to show that the basis for a general account of the symbol is present in Freud's work, albeit with a good deal of textual sifting and conceptual infilling; second, to show that this account is scientific.

On the first, Petocz argues that Freudian psychoanalysis contains two theories of symbolism. One is what she terms the narrow view, usually identified as the Freudian theory, even at times by Freud himself, and which is articulated most fully in the middle years of his work. The other is the broad view, more deeply embedded and so not as clearly discernible in Freud's work but, with careful exposition, cogent and better supported.

Henry Friedman MD

Henry Friedman

Henry Friedman  

Heinz Kohut's theories regarding disorders of narcissism initially appeared to be compatible with classical drive theory, but with the publication in 1977 of The Restoration of the Self, the revolutionary nature of his contributions became apparent. Since then, Kohut's theories, grouped under the title of self psychology, have enlivened the thinking of many psychoanalysts while distressing others who find his challenges to classical theory and technique anywhere from unacceptable to irrelevant. An orthodoxy that had steadfastly been maintained by the policy of traditional psychoanalytic journals, whose editors routinely excluded any articles that failed to meet the definition of classical ego psychology, began to crumble. Kohut's status within the American Psychoanalytic Association, as well as his initial indication that his theories applied only to a restricted group of patients suffering from narcissistic disorders, contributed to the publication of his articles. A revolution in psychoanalytic thought and practice had been launched, but its adoption was by no means complete. The passionate acceptance of new ideas by some has also generated extreme resistance in others and an attempt within the traditional literature to either incorporate these new ideas in a fashion that absorbs and neutralizes them as mere accessories to classical analysis or continues to attack them as the enemies of true analysis.

Henry J. Friedman, MD. Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; and independent practice, Cambridge, Massachusetts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Human Touch

An Exploration of the Role and Meaning of Physical Touch in Psychoanalysis

Ellen Toronto PHD

Ellen Toronto

Ann Arbor, Michigan

Ellen Toronto  

This article addressed the meaning and significance of minor physical contact (e.g., a hug or handshake) within the psychoanalytic dyad. Although undoubtedly a controversial topic, the plain fact that it is occurring makes touch an important and necessary subject of discourse. Furthermore, as psychoanalysts come to define the role of the more human analyst within the 2-person field and come to regard so-called extra-analytic techniques as necessary and even highly therapeutic, there is no logical basis from which to exclude the judicious use of actual physical contact as one of the tools of the more “human” analyst. The author presented clinical material in which touch seemed ill-advised as well as that in which it proved efficacious. She thus endeavored to clarify and bring into conscious awareness those factors by which decisions are made to touch or not to touch as well as how. when, and with what types of patients psychoanalysts decide to have physical contact.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Temperament and Dyadic Contributions to Affect Regulation

Implications From Developmental Research for Clinical Practice

Eva Appelman PHD

Eva Appelman

Eva Appelman  

The temperamental trait of inhibition offers a setting to examine the characteristics of the interplay between intraindividual factors and contextual influences. This article drew from both the developmental psychoanalytic model on mutual regulation of affect and developmental research on temperament to portray the mechanisms and consequences of the dynamic processes of change, in which both the individual and the environment are constantly changing. The portrait presents a unique case of a very inhibited girl at age 3, who gradually shifts from a frozen, fearful stance into a spontaneous, active behavior. The examination uses the paradigm of coconstruction of play narratives, a paradigm that combines both projective techniques and developmental research perspectives. The function of the adults as stress buffers is explored.

Eva Appelman, PhD, Cambridge Hospital. Cambridge, Massachusetts, and Harvard Medical School.

This work has been done with the support of Dennie Wolf, Jerome Kagan, and Nancy Snidman. I am grateful to them for their inspiration in bringing this work to fruition. Thanks to Bennet Simon and Thomas Rosbrow for their careful reading and comments on earlier drafts of this article.

[[RunningHead]]

Relationality: From Attachment to Intersubjectivity

Stephen Mitchell, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 2000, 173 pp., $39.95.

Timothy Zeddies PHD

Timothy Zeddies

Timothy Zeddies  

To love in a committed fashion, over time, is to hope; and to hope is to impart value in an inevitably uncertain future. Both love and hope are extremely risky; a genuine hopefulness about the analytic process is not possible in any real way at the beginning but is something attained only through struggle; it takes great courage (Mitchell, 1993, p. 212)

As a graduate student, I became enamored very quickly with psychoanalysis. After several years of reading everything traditional and post-Freudian I could get my hands on, I happened unexpectedly to run across Hope and Dread in Psychoanalysis (Mitchell, 1993), which I read and then reread in the same week. For me, this book was (and remains) one of the most clearly written, encompassing, and compelling portraits of psychoanalysis. Soon thereafter, when I had consumed everything its author had written, I began to write my dissertation and sought to make an exploration of his work its centerpiece. I wanted to include his latest writings in my project, and so I held my breath and called him directly to ask if he would send me any of his recent writings. To my surprise, he called back in a couple days, offered to send me a manuscript he was working on (which became Influence and Autonomy in Psychoanalysis; Mitchell, 1997) and invited me to comment on it, as well as to send him what I had written. I was stunned when I heard the message, and played it over several times just to be sure that I had not completely imagined it. As I got to know Steve better over the next 5 years, I was always impressed by how open.

Timothy J. Zeddies, PhD, Department of Psychology, University of the incarnate Word.

Stuart A. Pizer, in Building Bridges: The Negotiation of Paradox In Psychoanalysis, outlines the relational model of psychoanalysis by defining its major elements (negotiation and paradox) and the parallel mechanisms by which he identifies the classical, drive-centered model of psychoanalysis (conflict and interpretation). Pizer presents his hypotheses in one major case study (the case of “Donald”) and in a number of less fully rendered case studies. In addition to explicating some of the major elements of the relational and classical models, Pizer makes it clear that he favors the relational as the more democratic model of treatment and, according to Pizer, the model that encourages the patient's purported desires and needs for equality with and autonomy in relation to the presumably more egalitarian practitioner of the relational method.

I first turn to the conflict model, which has been the major vehicle for representing the patient's presenting problem(s). The conflict model presents the patient's problem(s) as internal (e.g., a conflict between the patient's drives, in the id, and the patient's defenses, in the ego), external (e.g., involving a battle between the patient's desires, or id, and values, or superego, and the dictates of society—the external world) or both. The conflict is presented by Pizer as a choice of one of two alternatives—a decision of either one or the other alternative—whereas the model of paradox offers a choice among a number of alternatives, the decision permitting a choice of both alternatives or of either one. Molly Jones-Quinn  

The contributors to this volume of 10 essays on the erotic elements range in their concept of “erotic” from passionate genital longings to a mutually bestowed gift of tenderness. Editor David Mann acknowledges that the collection is not exhaustive, but is broadly illustrative of the fact that diverse schools of contemporary psychoanalytic theory—drive, object relations, self, Kleinian, Jungian, and Lacanian theoretical perspectives— converge on some widely agreed on points relating to erotic phenomena. All the authors agree that evoking the incestuous erotic underpinnings of the transference-countertransference closeness is essential to promoting deep psychological growth, clearly distinguishing this healthy capacity for closeness, love, and sexual desire from perverse and psychotic eroticized manifestations.

These 10 essays, all intellectually stimulating and emotionally inspiring, collectively represent the notion that love is the sine qua non of a successful psychoanalysis. There are complex variations of meaning in the term “love,” whether it is a generic commitment on the part of one who chooses to be an analyst and an inevitable response from a “good” patient or the result of an evolving relationship based on mutual respect and a “good-enough fit.” The manifestations of love are explored as related to mythological and classical concepts, its boundaries and fluctuations presumed, but not tied to an exploration of an unfolding therapeutic process (Novick and Novick, 2000). Language and the Body

The Talking Cure in the New Century

Luca Di Donna PHD

Luca Di Donna

San Francisco, California

Luca Di Donna  

This panel was the second plenary meeting entitled “History and Psychoanalysis.” The 3 invited speakers were Robert Wallerstein, Charles Spezzano, and Hanna Decker. Dr. Wallerstein discussed his current views of psychoanalysis, pointing out that there are areas of common ground within contemporary psychoanalytic theories. Dr. Spezzano suggested that there are 3 sites in the analytic situation: free association, reveries, and enactments in which the analyst gathers information that structure his or her understanding of technique. Professor Decker spoke about hysteria from a historical perspective and discussed its manifestation in somatic patients.

Robert Wallerstein, MD, presented a paper on “The Trajectory of Psychoanalysis Past and Future.” Wallerstein, a leading voice in American and international psychoanalysis, has followed the many changes that have occurred in psychoanalysis in the past 50 years. The paper presented his views on the history and future of psychoanalysis.

He summarized the beginning of psychoanalysis by pointing out that there was a constant concern for its future and a fight with both the internal and external enemy to establish itself as recognized discipline.

Luca Di Donna, PhD, independent practice, San Francisco, California.

responseSet.title: Vitalizing Objects and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vitalizing Objects and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Ryan LaMothe PHD

Ryan LaMothe

Ryan LaMothe  

Patients, with some measure of unconscious hope and dread, seek vitalizing objects. These objects, which represent interactional processes, provide persons with subjective and intersubjective experiences of being alive and real. This article proposes that a constitutional structure of vitality, which is initially nonpropositional and preconceptual, is projected onto objects, rituals, and persons. Ideally this provides persons with subjective and intersubjective organizations and meanings of being alive and real. The development, transformation, and expansion of these organizations and experiences are contingent on the complex interplay of trusted significant figures, primary and secondary transitional phenomena, and the person's constitutional capacities. Patients often display varying degrees of constricted abilities to make use of shared objects to feel alive and real. A goal of therapy is facilitating the patient's capacity to risk creating, projecting onto, and making use of socially shared phenomena for more diverse and expansive organizations and meanings of feeling alive and real.

[[RunningHead]]

Cartesian and Post-Cartesian Trends in Relational Psychoanalysis

Robert Stolorow PHD , Donna Orange PHD PsyD and George Atwood PHD

George Atwood

George Atwood  

This article illuminates the ways in which relational psychoanalysis has remained caught in the grip of the Cartesian, isolated-mind philosophy it has sought to subvert. Residual Cartesianism is examined in the work of Sullivan and Fair-bairn, in “present-moment” thinking, in the mutual-recognition model of intersubjectivity, in the concept of projective identification, and in the mixed models prevalent in relational theorizing. The aim of this critique is to move relational theory toward becoming ever more contextual.

One of the greatest difficulties encountered in bringing about favorable change is this almost inescapable illusion that there is a perduring, unique, simple existent self, [which is] in some strange fashion, the patient's, or the subject person's, private property.

—H. S. Sullivan (1950, p. 329)

Robert D. Stolorow, PhD, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles, California; Donna M. Orange, PhD, PsyD, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York, New York; George E. Atwood, PhD, Department of Psychology, Rutgers. The State University of New Jersey.

responseSet.title: From Cartesian Minds to Experiential Worlds in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Cartesian Minds to Experiential Worlds in Psychoanalysis

Donna Orange PHD PsyD

Donna Orange

Donna Orange  

Eight contrasts are drawn between the characteristics of the Cartesian mind and the assumptions embedded in the alternative conception of an experiential world. The Cartesian mind is isolated and self-sufficient, subject opposed to object, inner, devoted to clear-and-distinct ideas, reliant on true-false logic, atemporal, representational, and substantial. The experiential world is relational, social, dialogic, and contextual, perspectival, doubly inhabited and inhabiting, complex and fallibilistic, more or less aware, temporal and emergent, understanding-oriented, and organizing process. Some practical consequences of these contrasts are briefly mentioned.

[[RunningHead]]

Integrative Treatment of Symptomatic Disorders

Mary Connors PHD

Mary Connors

Mary Connors  

Symptomatic disorders such as substance abuse, eating disorders, depression, and anxiety states can cause significant life impairment in patients. The author proposed that an optimally responsive, analytically oriented treatment for an individual with symptomatic distress may involve the use of active symptom-focused techniques. Reduction of symptomatic problems strengthens the self and facilitates deeper levels of self-exploration and therapeutic involvement. Techniques originating in behavioral and cognitive-behavioral orientations may be implemented in an analytically informed treatment and adapted accordingly. Current psychoanalytic models such as self psychology and some other relational approaches that emphasize the importance of empathic appreciation of a patient's perspective and optimal responsiveness to the individual can encompass such an integrative approach.

responseSet.title: What Freudians Can Learn From Jung
responseSet.authorMast: John Beebe,  Joseph Cambray and  Thomas B. Kirsch
responseSet.authorCitation: Beebe, J., Cambray, J. & Kirsch, T. B.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Freudians Can Learn From Jung

John Beebe MD , Joseph Cambray PHD and Thomas Kirsch MD

Thomas Kirsch

California

Providence, Rhode Island; and Boston, Massachusetts

California

Thomas Kirsch  

Noting that there has been an increasing sharing of ideas between psychoanalysts and analytical psychologists within the past decade, the authors, all Jungian analysts, survey Jung's clinical theory in an effort to identify areas where psychoanalytic clinicians might find Jung's concepts helpful. They discuss Jung's approach to the dream, his understanding of psychotherapy as a dialectical procedure, and the theory of complexes and archetypes. They also review how Jung's notions of the Self and individuation are used in contemporary Jungian practice. A dream of a man in his mid-30s is presented to illustrate how these formulations can facilitate understanding of the patient and the analytic process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretation of Dreams

Sigmund Freud, translated by Joyce Crick, with an Introduction and Notes by Ritchie Robertson, Oxford, England: Oxford University Press, 1999, liv + 458 pp., $27.50

G. Pigman PHD

G. Pigman

G. Pigman  

Freud was proud of The Interpretation of Dreams. In the preface to the third English edition (1932), he called it “the most valuable of all the discoveries it has been my good fortune to make” (Freud, 1900/1953b, p. xxxii). Convinced of its central importance to his work, he revised and expanded six of the eight editions that appeared during his lifetime. These revisions go far beyond the well-known additions concerning the extent and significance of symbolism in dreams, as Grubrich-Simitis (2000) has demonstrated so well. Even if one does not agree with Ritchie Robertson that “the original dream theory is based on a much more flexible and sensitive interpretation of dream imagery” (p. xxxviii), it is still instructive to read the considerably shorter Interpretation of Dreams that Freud sent into the world in November 1899. Because the publication of this translation coincided with the publication of a facsimile edition of the German first edition (Freud, 1900/1999), readers can now easily compare Joyce Crick's work with the original.

Strachey's notes in the Standard Edition have allowed readers to reconstruct most of the changes in the later editions, but Crick's translation reveals that Strachey did not record “every alteration of substance introduced into the book since its first issue” (Freud, 1900/1953b, p. xx). Freud came to regard this book as part of his self-analysis, and a large part of its fascination stems from its autobiographical revelations, as intimate as anything in works that he published.

Malignant Narcissism on the Screen

Isaac Tylim PsyD

Isaac Tylim

Isaac Tylim  

Am. Psychol. (1999) may be described as a vivid screen illustration of malignant narcissism. Adapted from Bret Easton Ellis's (1991/2000) eponymous novel, the film was elegantly directed by Mary Harron, whose previous work includes I Shot Andy Warhol (1996). Harron's films transfer the classical American myth of individualism and self-sufficiency—so often depicted in Westerns—to the urban landscape. Instead of cowboys, her films present creatures that roam around free, not in the big empty spaces of the American West, but the overbuilt and menacing jungle of America's big cities with their canyons-streets of cement. The conquest of the West is now the conquest of the Metropolis, and the brave and lone ranger of yesterday has been replaced by the greedy and lone narcissist of today.

Interviews with 72 mothers and their young adult daughters provided a transgenerational view of daughters' separation from mothers in early adulthood. Young adult daughters of mothers who remembered their own mothers as enabling autonomy described interdependent mother–daughter relationships based on mutual support. In contrast, daughters whose mothers gave incoherent accounts of their own mother's efforts to constrain autonomy strivings were highly dependent on their mothers. Finally, daughters whose mothers coherently described their own mothers as constraining were stridently independent,

responseSet.title: The Future of Psychoanalytic Institutes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Future of Psychoanalytic Institutes

Douglas Kirsner PHD

Douglas Kirsner

Douglas Kirsner  

Both conceptual and institutional problems permeate psychoanalytic institutes. Although institutional problems are historically based, they also derive from confusions around ill-defined concepts that lead to arbitrariness, authoritarianism, and the stifling of creativity. Psychoanalysis is a humanistic discipline that is touted as a science but is organized as a religion. Problems surrounding the right to train pervade psychoanalytic schisms, and transmission comes through processes of anointment. Institutional “false expertise” invokes the aura of anointment where training analysts pass down received truth through an esoteric pipeline depending on genealogy instead of function. Quasireligious thinking and politics rush in to fill the gap between the level of claimed knowledge that affords qualification and the far lower level of real knowledge. Institutes should rely on evidence of candidates' performance and engage in open-ended inquiry.

A Response to Commentaries by Silverman (2000) and Mitchell (2000)

Lawrence Josephs PHD

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs  

Psychoanalysis should aspire to scientific standards of openness about its evidence and accountability in regard to its assertions. Regardless of the inherent value of rigorous thinking cross-checked through clinical experience, it does not replace the need for verification that is independent of private clinical experience. In the end, psychoanalytic assertions possess limited credibility without independent verification.

responseSet.title: Subjectivity, Emotional Resonance, and the Sense of the Real
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Subjectivity, Emotional Resonance, and the Sense of the Real

William Coburn PHD PsyD

William Coburn

William Coburn  

The assumption of being inextricably embedded in one's own subjective perspective, on the one hand, and yet of being able to attune affectively to the subjective emotional states of another, on the other hand, underscores central theoretical contradictions in the study of subjectivity and intersubjectivity. More closely examining the nature and experience of subjectivity, including the notion of human “sameness” and relatedness, provides one way to reconcile these theoretical and practical contradictions. It is concluded that one's embeddedness in one's subjectivity actually facilitates the reciprocal and mutual processes of emotional resonance and unconscious communication. In this light, the notion of the “sense of the real” is understood and elaborated in the context of an additional dimension of therapeutic action in psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Talking Cure in the 21st Century Telephone Psychoanalysis

Arlene Richards PHD

Arlene Richards

New York

Arlene Richards  

Is psychoanalytic treatment by telephone a standard practice for psychologists? If it has value, in what situations is it effective? A survey of 120 members of Division 39 of the American Psychological Association showed that 83% had used it over the previous 2 years: 84% said they found such sessions usually productive, and 98% found them productive at least some of the time. Geography was the most frequent indication. A therapist or a patient moving to a new location and a patient traveling for work were the usual causes. Case studies showed that telephone work was especially useful in uncovering patient need for emotional distance and patient shame about physical characteristics.

responseSet.title: Case Reports in a Two-Person World
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Case Reports in a Two-Person World

Donald Spence PHD

Donald Spence

Donald Spence  

Traditional anecdotal case reports are usually written as a 1 st-person narrative, a tale told from the point of view of the treating analyst. Quotations from the patient are sometimes included, but readers rarely have access to the thoughts of either analyst or patient. Perhaps it is because of these omissions that the literature of case reports and transcribed recordings has uncovered only a few examples of persuasive interventions and has not contributed much to the understanding of what exactly brings about change in the course of psychoanalytic treatment. Perhaps the meanings readers seek are not in the published narrative but in the patient's and analyst's contexts of understanding—and these are left largely unrecorded. If ways were found to capture this context, analysts might better understand the principal ingredients of significant clinical happenings.

Postmodern approaches to the world (see Kirshner, 1999; Slife, 1999) are coming gradually to agree that the search for timeless and immutable laws in the study of the mind may be a fruitless project. Slife wrote that

in traditional science, the ultimate foundation for reality is considered to be natural laws….

Donald P. Spence, PhD, Department of Psychiatry, Robert wood Johnson Medical School, University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Female Sexuality: Contemporary Engagements

Donna Bassin (Ed.), Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1999, 507 pp., $60.00.

Helen Gediman PHD

Helen Gediman

Helen Gediman  

The reader of this unique volume can feast on a well-selected body of literature that highlights the evolution of psychoanalytic views that cluster around old and new controversies on female sexuality. Fifteen contemporary contributors each selected a classic paper believed to be generative on the subject of female sexuality and gender issues. Each of the 15 offer a commentary, a substantive essay in its own right, that introduces the classic selected, reexamines it from a contemporary point of view, and “maps out the contributor's specific stepping-stones, from transmission of the inherited work to her or his now-generative reading” (p. xi). On my reading, 15 classical muses inspired 15 contemporary writers to do one fine job.

Donna Bassin, the editor of this series, expresses an abiding faith in the generative power of classics, “which are living histories and remain dynamic as long as readers become writers from them” (p. xiii). The contributors and their thoughtfully selected classics do not disappoint. This gripping and fascinating volume conveys an appreciation of the history of ideas on its subject and should generate among its readers ever more new and communicable ideas. In writing this review, I imagined myself in a position similar to that of the 15 contributors. This very book took on the proportions of a classic-to-be and certainly inspired in me new and, I hope, communicable ideas that form the substance of this review. Unlike many edited books, this one is an integrated, creative, consistent, and coherent whole.

Helen K. Gediman, PhD. The New York Freudian Society, New York, New York.

Tales From the Couch: Writers on Therapy

Jason Shinder, New York: Morrow, 2000, xv + 220 pp., $24.00.

Jeffrey Berman PHD

Jeffrey Berman

Jeffrey Berman  

From its inception, psychoanalysis has been indebted to literature: Freud sought confirmation of his theories in classical and contemporary drama, poetry, and fiction; and creative writers have reciprocated not only by becoming analysands but also by writing about their experiences with the talking cure. The history of the relationship between psychoanalysis and literature has been fascinating and sometimes turbulent. Many creative writers have described, in fictional and nonfictional works, their experiences with therapy and its effect on their art, but curiously, we do not have, in a single volume, writers speaking about this subject in their own words. It is for this reason that Tales From the Couch is a welcome addition.

Tales From the Couch consists of an all-too-brief Introduction by the editor, Jason Shinder, a poet who teaches in the graduate program at Bennington College and the New School for Social Research, and essays by seventeen creative writers who discuss their own therapy. The contributors explore a wide range of therapies, ranging from traditional psychoanalysis, discussed by Adam Gopnik, to the experimental strategy of eye movement desensitization and reprocessing experienced by Pam Houston. Nearly all of the contributors write about verbal therapy, and although their experiences differed widely, most found the process valuable both to their life and art. As Shinder notes in the Introduction, all of the contributors agree that “for effective, genuine therapy to occur, one must risk the uncertainty, fear, shame, and lack of control, real or imaginary, that come with true and ongoing self-revelation” (xiv).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Central Role of Pathogenic Expectations and Beliefs in a Case of Intense Genital Damage Anxiety

Suzanne Gassner PHD

Suzanne Gassner

Suzanne Gassner  

Pathogenic expectations and beliefs are constructions of reality, usually unconscious, that link frightening outcomes to the pursuit of normal developmental goals. These expectations, derived from past painful experiences, are conceptualized as a root cause for symptoms, repression, and other defenses. The first 5 years of an analysis are presented here to demonstrate how treatment technique is affected by an understanding of pathogenic expectations and beliefs as organizers of psychic reality and psychopathology. The major mutative force in this beneficial treatment was a process of gradually disconfirming the patient's expectations within a treatment relationship that the patient perceived as offering acceptance and empathic explanations of her psychic reality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Manic Dedifferentiation and the Creative Process

David Anderegg PHD and Gina Gartner BA

Gina Gartner

Gina Gartner  

For the past decade or more, researchers have found significant correlations between creative achievement and symptoms of mood disorders, especially bipolar disorder. While these findings have been intriguing, there has been little or no attempt at explanation, on a psychological level, for these correlations. This article sets forth a model for describing the experience of mania in the vocabulary of psychoanalytic developmental theory: psychological dedifferentiation, which is a restitutive response to loss and can be, at the same time, a cognitive substrate for creative mental activity. Case material from (a) a bipolar patient in psychotherapy, (b) a 2nd bipolar patient's psychological testing protocol, and (c) the work of a bipolar artist are presented in support of the link between manic dedifferentiation and creativity.

[[RunningHead]]

Humor and Psyche: Psychoanalytic Perspectives

James W. Barron, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 1999, xi + 232 pp., $45.00.

Jeffrey Berman PHD

Jeffrey Berman

Jeffrey Berman  

Jokes and Their Relation to the Unconscious remains one of Freud's most memorable books, yet psychoanalysts have been reluctant to discuss the role of humor in therapy. Even Freud, great collector of jokes that he was, had little to add about the subject after the publication of his joke book in 1905. Many psychoanalysts have agreed with Lawrence Kubie's (1971) influential judgment that the analyst's humor is inappropriate as a therapeutic technique. Given Kubie's solemn belief that an analyst's humor during therapy is an exhibitionistic defense against anxiety, it is understandable that psychoanalysts have been unwilling to publish articles and books extolling humor as a legitimate analytic technique. Humor, alas, is no laughing matter in therapy! A few analysts, most notably Warren Poland (1990), have recognized the gift of humor, but they have been the exception. It is for this reason that the publication of James Barron's edited volume Humor and Psyche is a welcome addition to psychoanalytic scholarship.

Humor and Psyche is divided into three sections: “Historical and Theoretical Perspectives,” “Therapeutic Process,” and “Character and Creativity.” The ten essays in the volume cover a wide range of subjects and reflect diverse strengths. Martin Bergmann's lead essay, “The Psychoanalysis of Humor and Humor in Psychoanalysis,” which is one of the best in the collection, notes that although analysts have been reluctant to go beyond Freud's analysis of humor, they are slowly beginning to recognize the healing aspects of humor.

Self-Reflexivity, Intersubjectivity, and Therapeutic Change

John Auerbach PHD and Sidney Blatt PHD

Sidney Blatt

Sidney Blatt  

Intersubjectivity theory holds that the self forms through relationships with others in a dialectic process. Although affect attunement is central to early self-other differentiation, infants do not see themselves as separate entities until 18 months and their minds as separate until approximately age 4. Transitional object relatedness thus often influences early childhood functioning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Language of the Body

Allusions to Self-Experience in Women's Poetry

Marilyn Charles PHD

Marilyn Charles

East Lansing, Michigan

Marilyn Charles  

The author uses the work of 2 female poets to explore women's representations of self and body in their art. In these works, one can see how woman's ambivalent relationship with the body impedes the ability to know what can only be known through grounding in one's experience. Sylvia Plath, in her 1962 poem “Three Women” (S. Plath, 1981), uses the frames of 3 disparate women, each standing at the uneasy precipice of motherhood, to express her multiplicity and deep ambivalence regarding the body-self. In contrast, Adrienne Rich's work reflects a greater peace with the body, embodying the ongoing conflict between body becoming mind and mind becoming body. Poetry invites the reader into the text through the use of anomaly and disjunction, which deconstructs ostensible meanings and invites personalized and affectively charged readings. Rich's work, most particularly, invites the reader toward the kind of transformative journey referred to by Bion as “transformations in ‘O.’” In this way, poetry, like analysis, helps to fragment occluded meanings, facilitating new, more cogent interpretations and integrations, thereby facilitating the task of becoming.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Barriers to Intimacy

An Object Relations Perspective

Richard Alperin DSW

Richard Alperin

Richard Alperin  

Intimacy has rarely been discussed in the psychoanalytic literature, although clinicians recognize that problems with intimacy are universal. In this article, intimacy is defined, and the socio-cultural factors that serve as obstacles to intimate relatedness are discussed. Closely examined are the intrapsychic conflicts that inhibit the development of intimacy, such as a fear of fusion, a fear of object loss, paranoid-schizoid anxieties, and sexual anxieties. Because analysts help to create an environment where problems of intimacy can best be studied, understood, and treated, implications for psychoanalytic treatment are discussed. A clinical example from the author's psychoanalytic practice is offered to illustrate some of the concepts discussed.

responseSet.title: The Generations of Psychotherapy Research: An Overview
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Generations of Psychotherapy Research

An Overview

Robert Wallerstein MD

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein  

Psychotherapy research can be divided into 4 generations, marked by increasing conceptual sophistication and technological enhancement. The 1st generation (1917-1968) comprised simple retrospective counts of improved outcomes by unspecified criteria. The 2nd generation (1959-1985) consisted of 2 kinds: (a) prospective group-aggregated studies of psychoanalytic treatments, with specified definitions, operationalized criteria, and predictions to expected outcomes, and (b) individually studied outcomes in a sequence of patients. The 3rd generation (1954-1986) combined both kinds of 2nd-generation studies with the added feature of separating outcomes at termination from the more enduring effects at subsequent follow-up. The 4th, current generation (1970s-) adds in micro-analytic process studies made possible by recent technological advances—audiotapes, combined with computer searches.

Robert S. Wallerstein, MD, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute, San Francisco, California, and University of California, San Francisco, School of Medicine.

This article was presented in slightly altered form as the Friday evening plenary lecture, Rapaport–Klein Study Group meeting at Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, Massachusetts, June 11, 1999, and as the plenary lecture at Section 6, Division 39, San Francisco, April 2000.

Correspondence concerning this article should be addressed to Robert S. Wallerstein, MD, 290 Beach Road, Belvedere, California 94920. Electronic mail may be sent to judywallerstein@msn.com.

No Escape When the Past Is Endless

Judith Alpert PHD

Judith Alpert

Judith Alpert  

An intricate and chilling rendering of traumatic memory is presented. Among other observations, the article indicates that loss of traumatic material begins early and that children work as ferociously to forget as adults do to remember. The author also indicates that trauma cannot be made mute and that memory demands a more complicated outlook than a simple true–false dichotomy. The article also suggests that there may be inconsistency in trauma communication, and it points to a whole web of defense mechanisms that trauma may impel.

[[RunningHead]]

Mourning and the Transformation of Object Relationships

Evidence for the Persistence of Internal Attachments

John Baker PHD

John Baker

John Baker  

Psychoanalysts from Freud up to the present have defined the goal of mourning as the detachment of libidinal ties from the deceased love object. The author reviewed recent clinical and empirical literature that casts doubt on this assumption by showing that a continuing internal relationship with the lost object is found in many bereaved individuals. These data suggest a need to reconceptualize the changes in object relationships that occur during the process of mourning. Mourning is seen as a process of inner transformation that affects both the images of the self and of the object in the mourner's inner world. It involves not the breaking of an object tie, but the transformation of that attachment into a sustaining internal presence, which operates as an ongoing component in the individual's internal world.

responseSet.title: The Impending Death of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impending Death of Psychoanalysis

Robert Bornstein PHD

Robert Bornstein

Robert Bornstein  

Although psychoanalysis once dominated psychology, evidence now points to the waning influence of psychoanalytic theory in psychological science, psychiatric diagnosis, undergraduate instruction, and graduate training. In this article I describe 7 self-destructive behaviors exhibited by psychoanalysts that contributed to the precipitous decline of psychoanalytic theory in recent years. I then outline three strategies for retaining those features of psychoanalysis that are scientifically and clinically useful while jettisoning those that are dated and inaccurate. These strategies might enable scientific psychologists and research-minded practitioners to reinvigorate psychoanalytic theory during the 21st century.

[[RunningHead]]

Obstacles to Integration Another Look at Why We Talk Past Each Other

Henry Smith MD

Henry Smith

Henry Smith  

This article, written before Stephen Mitchell's death, draws on Mitchell's (1997) Influence and Autonomy in Psychoanalysis to illustrate some common discursive strategies that tend to fracture analytic dialogue. Such strategies include marginalization of the past, the use of sharp polarities, excessively tight linkages between theory and technique, and argument from epistemology rather than clinical observation. The author suggests that the clinical situation is more complex than these positions imply, that the polarities are inherent in the work itself, and that theoretical differences do not necessarily define the work. He concludes with the hope that further dialogue, based on the description of what analysts observe themselves doing in their own clinical work, might begin to illuminate what they hold in common and what remains beyond integration.

At a recent gathering of psychoanalysts, Ronald Britton (1998), the contemporary British Kleinian, was asked if he listened carefully to other people's theories.

Henry F. Smith, MD, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East, Needham. Massachusetts.
